We are one of the world’s fastest-growing global colocation providers, extending and expanding our data center footprint into new markets as demand arises. Our data center platform includes 30+ data centers throughout 3 continents that enable and support the colocation, interconnection and services our customers need.

## Asia-Pacific data center locations

The Asia Pacific region is expected to account for half of the global colocation market by 2025. Our data center locations in Singapore and India give customers strategic regional reach and rich interconnection ecosystems, all with the uncompromising Iron Mountain approach to standards and services. Singapore straddles the sea cables connecting India, South East Asia, China, Japan and Australasia, while our facilities, and recent expansions in Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Pune, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Chennai offer significant capacity with unparalleled reach across India, the world’s second-largest telecommunications market. We are committed to a high standard of service and customer care and are pleased to support our customers throughout the APAC region.

[Get In Touch](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/contact-us)

![Data Centers Locations APAC map](https://www.ironmountain.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fedge.sitecorecloud.io%2Fironmountain-c8dd68e9%2Fmedia%2Fproject%2Firon-mountain%2Firon-mountain%2Fimages%2Fservices-and-industries%2Fimdc%2Fhero-and-background-over-800px%2Fapac-map.jpg%3Fh%3D550%26iar%3D0%26w%3D843%26sc_lang%3Den&w=1920&q=80)

## All APAC locations

[**Bangalore data center** \\
Enterprise colocation in Bangalore's (Bengaluru) tech hub. 4.1MW Tier III facility in Whitefield with 180+ ISPs and major cloud connectivity.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/bengaluru-data-center) [**Chennai Data Centers \| India Data Centers** \\
Our Chennai data center campus, located in Ambattur area, will provide 42 MW of power, with the first 23 MW expected at CHN-1 in 2026.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/chennai-data-center) [**Delhi Data Center \| Delhi-NCR Data Center** \\
Iron Mountain's Delhi-NCR data center offers reliable, cost-effective data center solutions that adapt to your business needs.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/delhi-ncr-data-center) [**Hyderabad Data Center \| India Colocation** \\
Tier III Hyderabad data center with 120,000 square feet of colocation space and 5.7 MW capacity. Secure, scalable infrastructure in India's technology hub.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/hyderabad-data-center) [**Mumbai Data Center \| Mumbai Colocation** \\
Iron Mountain's Mumbai data center offers reliable, cost-effective colocation and data centers in India that adapt to your business needs.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/mumbai-data-center) [**Pune Data Center \| India Colocation** \\
Iron Mountain's carrier-neutral Pune data center offers 38,000 square feet of secure colocation with 1.6 MW capacity in Maharashtra's tech hub.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/pune-data-center) [**Singapore data center** \\
Enterprise colocation in a premier Singapore data center. 7.3MW capacity, carrier-neutral, ABS OSPAR certified. Former Credit Suisse facility.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/singapore-data-center)

## Europe, Middle East & Africa data center locations

The EMEA region continues to see strong demand and rapid data center growth, especially in the core FLAP (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris) markets and emerging markets such as Madrid, Dublin and more. In response to that demand, we have been expanding our EMEA footprint, with major data center construction builds in Frankfurt, Amsterdam and London, Europe’s three leading colocation markets as well as in Madrid, one of Europe’s emerging markets. We have capacity throughout our EMEA data center footprint to meet the needs of our single cabinet customers to our most demanding hyperscale clients. Backed by robust connectivity options, high-touch customer support and industry leading sustainability and compliance certifications, we are well positioned to meet our EMEA customers needs.

[Get In Touch](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/contact-us)

## All EMEA locations

[**Amsterdam data center** \\
Iron Mountain's Amsterdam data center colocation facilities are located in Haarlem, Netherlands, deep in the heart of one of the world’s largest network ecosystems.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/amsterdam-data-center) [**Frankfurt data centers** \\
Iron Mountain’s Frankfurt data centers are located right in the heart of the desirable FLAP region in Western Europe](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/frankfurt-data-center) [**London data centers** \\
Iron Mountain’s London data center campus is located in the UK data center interconnection hub of Slough, in close proximity to the large peering exchanges.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/london-data-center) [**Madrid data centers** \\
Iron Mountain’s Madrid data center, acquired via XData Properties, brings our data center business to Spain](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/madrid-data-center) [**Qatar data center partnership for MENA region** \\
Iron Mountain enters into a strategic minority partnership in Qatar with Ooredoo, unlocking access to the North Africa and Middle East data center region.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/syntys-data-center)

## North America data center locations

Our North American data center footprint consists of data centers in 11 strategic markets. Our footprint includes enterprise, edge and hyperscale data centers in: Boston, Chicago, Denver, Kansas City, Miami, New Jersey, Ohio, Northern Virginia, Pennsylvania, Phoenix, Richmond and Scottsdale. Our facilities are certified by one of the industry’s most comprehensive compliance standards (Global ISO 50001, ISO 140001, FISMA High, FedRAMP and others) making our data centers ideal for regulated markets. As industry leaders in sustainability commitments, including [The Climate Pledge](https://www.theclimatepledge.com/) and the United Nations [24/7 Carbon-Free Energy (CFE) Compact](https://gocarbonfree247.com/), we are uniquely positioned to partner with customers who have sustainable and green goals.

[Get In Touch](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/contact-us)

Coming soon, Iron Mountain’s AI-ready Miami data center will support 16 MW of Miami colocation and data center space.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/miami-data-center) [**New Jersey data center** \\
Iron Mountain's NJE-1 New Jersey data center in Edison: 830k sq ft, 30MW power, 100% renewable energy, 24/7 Smart Hands support, 20+ carriers, near NYC](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/new-jersey-data-center) [**Ohio data center** \\
Our Ohio data center in Miamisburg offers enterprise colocation, modular data centers, and secure connectivity. Serving Cincinnati, Columbus, Dayton.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/ohio-data-center) [**Western Pennsylvania data center** \\
Ultra-secure underground Western Pennsylvania data center. Award-winning energy efficiency, comprehensive compliance, carrier-neutral connectivity.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/pennsylvania-data-center) [**Phoenix data centers** \\
Iron Mountain’s Phoenix, AZ data centers are located in the heart of one of the fastest-growing data center and colocation markets in the US.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/phoenix-data-center) [**Richmond data centers** \\
Coming soon, our Richmond data center campus will offer huge growth potential with 200+ MW of data center space at full build](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/richmond-data-center) [**Scottsdale data center** \\
Iron Mountain’s Scottsdale data center is located in one of the fastest-growing colocation markets in the US, situated near AZP-1 & AZP-2.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/scottsdale-data-center) [**Northern Virginia Data Centers** \\
Our N. Virginia data centers offer huge growth potential with multiple colocation facilities on campus. VA data centers are ideal for highly regulated organizations.](https://www.ironmountain.com/data-centers/locations/virginia-data-center)

The global data center market is currently experiencing rapid growth and is projected to continue its upward trajectory in the coming years. The market generated significant revenue in 2023. It is estimated that the global data center market size reached around USD 174 billion, with a projected growth rate of approximately 4% during that year. This substantial revenue growth can be attributed to the increasing demand for data storage and processing capabilities across various industries.

Factors such as digital transformation, cloud computing, and the proliferation of connected devices have contributed to the expansion of the data center market. Additionally, advancements in technology and the need for efficient data management have been driving factors for the growth in revenue.

With the continuous growth of data-driven technologies, it is expected that the global data center market will continue to expand in the coming years. According to industry reports, the market is expected to expand at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) ranging from 6.12% to 10.9%, depending on different sources, between 2023 and 2030.

The demand for enterprise data center services has been driven by a wide range of industries that recognize the importance of robust and reliable data infrastructure. One prominent industry is the technology sector, which relies heavily on [data centers](https://www.datacenters.com/locations) to support the storage, processing, and analysis of vast amounts of data generated by software development, cloud computing, and emerging technologies like artificial intelligence and machine learning.

The financial sector is another significant contributor, as banks, investment firms, and insurance companies require secure and high-performance data centers to handle sensitive financial transactions, manage customer data, and ensure regulatory compliance. Healthcare organizations also heavily rely on data centers to store electronic medical records, conduct research, and enable telehealth services.

E-commerce and retail companies utilize data centers to manage their online platforms, handle inventory management, and process customer transactions. The media and entertainment industry leverages data centers to manage content distribution, streaming services, and digital advertising operations.

No matter the technology that you're deploying or the industry that you operate in, it is crucial to place your data in the right geographical location to ensure optimal performance, security, and compliance. The choice of data center location can impact factors such as latency, network connectivity, data sovereignty, and disaster recovery capabilities.

As businesses increasingly rely on data-driven operations and cloud services, choosing the right data center has never been more vital for efficient and reliable data storage, processing, and accessibility. However, this surge in colocation demand also poses significant challenges, as the availability of data center space becomes scarcer.

# Top 10: Data Centre Companies in Africa

By [Ben Craske](https://datacentremagazine.com/author/ben-craske)

September 24, 2025

5 mins

Share this article

[**Prioritise Us** on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=datacentremagazine.com)

Share this article

[**Prioritise Us** on Google](https://google.com/preferences/source?q=datacentremagazine.com)

![](https://assets.bizclikmedia.net/138/88202d1220c8ef5c479d9e9362b49c38:cad00f00181568602d74f5d49689f074/datacentre-top10-companies-africa-cms-sep-2025.webp)

This week we spotlight the Top 10 Data Centre Companies in Africa

Meet Africa’s data centre giants – including Teraco, Africa Data Centres, MainOne and Rack Centre – that are unlocking the continent’s digital future

Africa’s [digital transformation](https://datacentremagazine.com/news/googles-investment-in-african-ai-infrastructure-explained) is accelerating, powered by a rapidly expanding network of world-class data centres.

The continent’s largest operators are setting new benchmarks in reliability, connectivity, and cloud neutrality, driving productivity and innovation across sectors from finance to entertainment.

From South Africa’s established colocation giants to Nigeria’s bold pioneers and pan-African specialists, these Top 10 Data Centres in Africa are critical pillars sustaining millions of connected lives and businesses across the continent’s [emerging digital economy](https://datacentremagazine.com/news/airtel-starts-work-on-44mw-east-africa-data-centre-hub).

## 10\. N+ONE Datacenters

- **Employees:** ~77
- **CEO:** Amine Kandil
- **Year founded:** 2008
- **Location:** Morocco

Credit: N+ONE Datacenters

N+ONE is Morocco’s leading carrier-neutral data centre operator, renowned for its Tier III facility in Casablanca.

The company delivers advanced colocation, hosting and cloud services to Moroccan and global enterprises, emphasising uptime and mission-critical support.

N+ONE’s highly secured infrastructure offers expert 24/7 management, robust connectivity, and efficient cooling and backup systems – making it a cornerstone for the country’s digital growth.

## 9\. Rack Centre

- **Employees:** ~68
- **CEO:** Lars Christer Johannisson
- **Year founded:** 2012
- **Location:** Nigeria

![Youtube Placeholder](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/fk2uQMljSfY/hqdefault.jpg)

According to its website, Rack Centre is Africa’s only carrier neutral Tier III Constructed Facility Certified data centre, based in Lagos, Nigeria.

Boasting a market-leading ecosystem, it supports more than 40 telecom carriers and operates a new 12MW AI-ready facility.

Rack Centre is recognised for its innovation, reliability and unyielding commitment to secure colocation and managed services, driving West Africa’s digital backbone.

## 8\. Open Access Data Centres (OADC)

- **Employees:** ~60
- **CEO:** Ayotunde Coker
- **Year founded:** 2021
- **Location:** Mauritius

Ayotunde Coker, CEO of OADC (Credit: ITW Africa)

OADC, a WIOCC Group company, is deploying Africa’s fastest-growing network of Tier III hyperscale and edge data centres.

The carrier- and cloud-neutral platform is pivotal for pan-African connectivity, with sites at cable landing stations and business hubs.

OADC’s mission is to enable Africa’s digital ecosystem with reliable infrastructure, localised solutions, and ESG-driven operations supporting service providers and content companies across the continent.

## 7\. NTT GDC (Global Data Centres, Africa)

- **Employees:** ~2,100 (NTT GDC total)
- **CEO:** Douglas Adams (NTT GDC)
- **Year founded:** 2018
- **Location:** South Africa

Douglas Adams, President and CEO, NTT Global Data Centers

[NTT’s Global Data Centres](https://datacentremagazine.com/company/ntt-data) in Africa, a segment of the Japanese multinational, provides industry-leading colocation and cloud solutions.

With hyperscale-ready infrastructure and a mature partner ecosystem, [NTT](https://datacentremagazine.com/company/ntt) has invested heavily in African connectivity, sustainability and skill development.

Its Johannesburg campus anchors ambitious expansion across the continent, consolidating NTT’s stature in secure, hybrid-ready environments for AI, cloud and enterprise IT.

## 6\. iXAfrica Data Centres

- **Employees:** ~43
- **CEO:** Snehar Shah
- **Year founded:** 2020
- **Location:** Kenya

Snehar Shah, CEO of iXAfrica Data Centres

As East Africa’s largest hyperscale, carrier-neutral, AI-ready data centre, iXAfrica operates out of Nairobi, Kenya.

Its flagship campus delivers robust colocation, cloud and connectivity to telecoms and digital service providers.

iXAfrica’s mission is to meet the region’s surging demand for edge and cloud computing, with future-forward power, cooling and network infrastructure designed for scale and resilience in emerging markets.

## 5\. PAIX Data Centres

- **Employees:** ~79
- **CEO:** Hemen Shah
- **Year founded:** 2016
- **Location:** Pan-African footprint (Accra HQ – Dutch parent)

Hemen Shah, CEO of PAIX Data Centres

PAIX is a pan-African carrier- and cloud-neutral data centre provider, recognised among Africa’s fastest-growing companies.

With hubs in Ghana, Kenya, Côte d'Ivoire and Senegal, PAIX delivers connectivity, colocation and cloud services to enterprises and service providers.

Its growth is driven by a commitment to secure operations, resilience and interconnectivity, and strategic leadership shaping Africa’s digital landscape.

## 4\. Raxio Group

- **Employees:** ~128
- **CEO:** Robert Skjødt
- **Year founded:** 2018
- **Location:** Pan-African (HQ in the Netherlands)

Robert Skjødt, CEO of Raxio Group

Raxio operates Africa’s most expansive network of Tier III carrier-neutral data centres, spanning Uganda to Ethiopia, Mozambique and Côte d’Ivoire.

Raxio’s facilities form the backbone for connectivity, cloud, and enterprise IT in emerging economies.

The group focuses on scaling responsibly, with sustainable practices and strategic expansion plans positioning it as a key enabler of Africa’s digital evolution.

## 3\. MainOne (Equinix)

- **Employees:** ~399
- **CEO:** Wole Abu (Managing Director of West Africa at Equinix)
- **Year founded:** 2010
- **Location:** Nigeria

Wole Abu, Managing Director of West Africa at Equinix

Acquired by [Equinix](https://datacentremagazine.com/company/equinix) in 2022, MainOne underpins West Africa’s digital transformation with leading connectivity and data centre services.

The company operates high-capacity cable and colocation facilities driving cloud integration and business innovation.

MainOne’s Lagos campus is pivotal for fintech, media and enterprise markets, capitalising on Equinix’s global footprint while maintaining regional leadership in connectivity, reliability and network edge services.

## 2\. Africa Data Centres

- **Employees:** ~143
- **CEO:** Adil El Youssefi
- **Year founded:** 2012
- **Location:** South Africa

![Youtube Placeholder](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/1dis22wGN3s/hqdefault.jpg)

Africa Data Centres is the continent’s largest interconnected, carrier and cloud-neutral data centre network, with presence across South Africa, Kenya, Nigeria, Ghana and more.

The company is celebrated for its state-of-the-art, purpose-built facilities, supporting hyperscale cloud providers, global carriers, and African enterprises.

With strategic investments from Cassava Technologies, ADC is leading the rise of Africa’s digital economy – championing secure, resilient and connected infrastructure for tomorrow’s markets.

## 1\. Teraco (Digital Realty)

- **Employees:** ~392
- **CEO:** Jan Hnizdo
- **Year founded:** 2008
- **Location:** South Africa

Teraco's JB4 facility in South Africa (Credit: Teraco)

Teraco, majority-owned by [Digital Realty](https://datacentremagazine.com/company/digital-realty), stands as Africa’s largest and most advanced carrier-neutral colocation provider.

With unmatched network density and resilient infrastructure, Teraco underpins South Africa’s enterprise and cloud backbone, attracting hyperscalers, telecoms and global corporations.

Its world-class, sustainable environments set a benchmark for reliability and digital transformation, with deep ties into the global cloud and data centre community via [Digital Realty’s platform](https://datacentremagazine.com/news/digital-realty-opens-third-lagos-data-centre).

Teraco leads by combining robust local expertise with global connectivity and transformation across the continent.

web.description: As of November 2025, the United States has 5,427 data centers, making it the world's largest data center market by a significant margin.
web.markdown:
RESEARCH # Number of Data Centers by Country (November 2025) [Rasmus Leichter](https://www.cargoson.com/en/blog/author/rasmus-leichter "View all articles by Rasmus Leichter")•Last updated: January 04, 2026•9 min read• Fact-checked Cite this research ## Research Highlights As of November 2025, **the United States has 5,427 data centers**, making it the world's largest data center market by a significant margin. Globally, there are approximately **12,000+ operational data centers**, with the U.S. accounting for 45% of all facilities worldwide. ## How many data centers does each country have? The United States dominates global data center infrastructure with **5,427 facilities** as of November 2025, according to Cloudscene's comprehensive data center database. This represents nearly half of all data centers worldwide. The top 10 countries by data center count are: | Rank | Country | Number of Data Centers | | --- | --- | --- | | 1 | United States | 5,427 | | 2 | Germany | 529 | | 3 | United Kingdom | 523 | | 4 | China | 449 | | 5 | Canada | 337 | | 6 | France | 322 | | 7 | Australia | 314 | | 8 | Netherlands | 298 | | 9 | Russia | 251 | | 10 | Japan | 222 | Top 10 Countries by Data Center Count (Nov 2025) ## Amount of data centers by region ### North America North America hosts **5,767 data centers**, representing approximately 48% of global facilities. The region is dominated by the United States. | Country | Data Centers | | --- | --- | | United States | 5,427 | | Canada | 337 | | Cayman Islands | 3 | | Total North America | 5,767 | Data Centers by Country: North America ### Europe Europe has **3,362 data centers** across 45 countries. Germany and the United Kingdom lead the region. | Country | Data Centers | | --- | --- | | Germany | 529 | | United Kingdom | 523 | | France | 322 | | Netherlands | 298 | | Russia | 251 | | Italy | 168 | | Poland | 144 | | Spain | 144 | | Switzerland | 121 | | Sweden | 95 | | Belgium | 81 | | Austria | 68 | | Ukraine | 58 | | Ireland | 55 | | Denmark | 50 | | Finland | 48 | | Norway | 47 | | Turkey | 35 | | Czech Republic | 34 | | Romania | 31 | | Latvia | 27 | | Bulgaria | 24 | | Luxembourg | 23 | | Portugal | 21 | | Lithuania | 18 | | Estonia | 16 | | Hungary | 15 | | Greece | 14 | | Slovakia | 14 | | Slovenia | 13 | | Serbia | 12 | | Croatia | 9 | | Belarus | 9 | | Moldova | 7 | | Cyprus | 6 | | Iceland | 6 | | Macedonia | 6 | | Malta | 6 | | Georgia | 4 | | Liechtenstein | 2 | | Monaco | 2 | | Gibraltar | 2 | | Bosnia and Herzegovina | 2 | | Azerbaijan | 1 | | Albania | 1 | | Total Europe | 3,346 | Data Centers by Country: Europe ### Asia-Pacific Asia-Pacific has **1,818 data centers**. China, Australia, and Japan are the regional leaders. | Country | Data Centers | | --- | --- | | China | 449 | | Australia | 314 | | Japan | 222 | | India | 153 | | Hong Kong | 122 | | Singapore | 99 | | Indonesia | 88 | | New Zealand | 83 | | Malaysia | 62 | | South Korea | 43 | | Thailand | 42 | | Philippines | 39 | | Vietnam | 33 | | Kazakhstan | 25 | | Taiwan | 19 | | Pakistan | 11 | | Bangladesh | 7 | | Cambodia | 7 | | Myanmar (Burma) | 6 | | Uzbekistan | 5 | | Papua New Guinea | 4 | | Kuwait | 4 | | Nepal | 3 | | Sri Lanka | 3 | | Iran | 2 | | Afghanistan | 1 | | Laos | 1 | | Bhutan | 1 | | Total Asia-Pacific | 1,818 | Data Centers by Country: Asia-Pacific ### South America South America has **654 data centers**, with Brazil and Mexico accounting for 57% of the region's facilities. | Country | Data Centers | | --- | --- | | Brazil | 197 | | Mexico | 173 | | Chile | 59 | | Colombia | 41 | | Argentina | 29 | | Peru | 21 | | Panama | 20 | | Puerto Rico | 19 | | Guatemala | 16 | | Ecuador | 15 | | Costa Rica | 13 | | El Salvador | 8 | | Honduras | 7 | | Uruguay | 6 | | Bolivia | 5 | | Nicaragua | 5 | | Curacao | 4 | | Venezuela | 4 | | Trinidad and Tobago | 4 | | Dominican Republic | 3 | | Bahamas | 3 | | Paraguay | 1 | | Jamaica | 1 | | Cuba | 0 | | Total South America | 654 | Data Centers by Country: South America ### Oceania Oceania has **401 data centers**, dominated by Australia. | Country | Data Centers | | --- | --- | | Australia | 314 | | New Zealand | 83 | | Papua New Guinea | 4 | | Total Oceania | 401 | Data Centers by Country: Oceania ## What is the global data center market size? The global data center market was valued at **$527.46 billion in 2025** and is projected to reach **$739.05 billion by 2030**, growing at a 6.98% compound annual growth rate (CAGR), according to Statista. However, data center infrastructure spending tells a more dramatic story. Gartner reported **$489.5 billion in data center systems spending for 2025**, representing a 46.8% increase from 2024's $333.4 billion. This explosive growth reflects the AI infrastructure boom. ### Data center market size by region (2025) **| Region | Market Size | % of Global Market |** **| --- | --- | --- |** **| United States | $171.90B | 33% |** **| Europe | $89.67B | 17% |** **| China | $84.39B | 16% |** **| Rest of World | $181.50B | 34% |** **| Global Total | $527.46B | 100% |** **Global Data Center Market Size by Region (2025)** ## How many hyperscale data centers exist globally? There are **1,189 large hyperscale data centers globally** as of Q1 2025, with 504 additional facilities in planning and construction stages, according to Synergy Research Group. The sector added 137 new hyperscale facilities in 2024 alone, with annual growth expected to continue at 130-140 new hyperscale data centers per year. ### Amount of hyperscale data centers by country (top 10) **| Country | Hyperscale DCs | % of Global Total |** **| --- | --- | --- |** **| United States | 642 | 54% |** **| China | 190 | 16% |** **| Japan | 71 | 6% |** **| Germany | 60 | 5% |** **| United Kingdom | 48 | 4% |** **| Australia | 36 | 3% |** **| Canada | 30 | 3% |** **| Netherlands | 24 | 2% |** **| Singapore | 21 | 2% |** **| Ireland | 18 | 2% |** **| Other countries | 49 | 3% |** **| Global Total | 1,189 | 100% |** **Hyperscale Data Centers by Country (Top 10)** **Note:** Hyperscale data centers are defined as facilities operated by hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, Alibaba, Tencent, etc.) that are typically larger than 10,000 square feet and consume more than 1 megawatt of power. ## What is the total global data center capacity? Global data center capacity reached **122.2 gigawatts (GW)** of installed IT power capacity as of Q1 2025, according to Synergy Research Group. ### Data center GW capacity distribution by region **| Region | IT Power Capacity | % of Global Total |** **| --- | --- | --- |** **| United States | 53.7 GW | 44% |** **| Europe | 20.8 GW | 17% |** **| China | 19.6 GW | 16% |** **| Asia-Pacific (ex-China) | 16.9 GW | 14% |** **| Latin America | 6.1 GW | 5% |** **| Other | 5.1 GW | 4% |** **| Global Total | 122.2 GW | 100% |** **Global Data Center IT Power Capacity by Region (Q1 2025)** Hyperscale operators currently control **44% of global data center capacity**, up from just 20% in 2017. This share is projected to reach **61% by 2030**. ## How much do companies spend on AI data centers? The four largest hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta) collectively plan to spend over **$370 billion on data center infrastructure in 2025**, up from $244 billion in 2024. ### AI and cloud infrastructure spending by company (2025) | Company | 2025 Spending | 2026 Estimate | Primary Focus | Source | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Amazon (AWS)** | $125 billion | ~$126–130B | Cloud & AI infrastructure | [Amazon raised spending to $125B in Q3 2025](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/10/30/amazon-amzn-q3-earnings-report-2025.html), up from earlier $118B estimate | | **Microsoft** | $88.7 billion (FY2025\*) | $120B+ (FY2026\*\*) | AI-enabled data centers | [Microsoft spent $88.7B in fiscal 2025](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/big-techs-ai-investments-set-to-spike-to-364-billion-in-2025-as-bubble-fears-ease-143203885.html);
[Q1 FY2026 was $30B+](https://markets.financialcontent.com/wral/article/marketminute-2025-9-15-microsofts-30-billion-ai-bet-a-deep-dive-into-its-cloud-and-ai-leadership-ambitions) | | **Google (Alphabet)** | $91–93 billion | Higher (TBA) | AI, cloud, infrastructure | [Alphabet raised 2025 capex; significant increase in 2026](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/10/29/google-expects-significant-increase-in-capex-in-2026-execs-say.html) | | **Meta** | $66–72 billion | ~$100 billion | AI training infrastructure | [Meta expects $66–72B in 2025](https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/30/meta-to-spend-up-to-72b-on-ai-infrastructure-in-2025-as-compute-arms-race-escalates/);
[could hit $100B by 2026](https://www.fusionww.com/insights/resources/the-cost-of-ai-how-hyperscaler-spending-is-impacting-semiconductor-supply) | | Total | ~$371–379 billion | ~$446B+ | | | AI & Cloud Infrastructure Spending by Company (2025)\\* Microsoft’s fiscal year ends June 30 \\*\\* Microsoft’s FY2026 started July 1, 2025 McKinsey projects that **$6.7 trillion in cumulative capital expenditure will be required globally between 2025-2030** to meet data center demand, with $5.2 trillion dedicated to AI-specific infrastructure. ## What are the largest recent data center investments? The data center industry is experiencing unprecedented investment, with several projects exceeding $10 billion. ### Major data center investments announced 2024-2025 | Project | Investment | Location | Company | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Stargate Initiative | $500B | Multiple U.S. sites | OpenAI/Oracle/SoftBank | | Aligned Data Centers acquisition | $40B | U.S. (50+ campuses) | BlackRock consortium | | AWS Georgia expansion | $35B | Georgia, USA | Amazon | | Reliance AI Campus | $30B | Jamnagar, India | Reliance Industries | | Microsoft UK commitment | $30B | United Kingdom | Microsoft | | Brookfield/Data4 AI infrastructure | $21B | France | Brookfield/Data4 | | AWS Project Rainier | $11B | Indiana, USA | Amazon | | AWS Mississippi complex | $10B | Mississippi, USA | Amazon | | AWS Pennsylvania expansion | $10B | Pennsylvania, USA | Amazon | | Meta Hyperion project | $10B | Louisiana, USA | Meta | Major Data Center Investments Announced (2024–2025) ## How much electricity do data centers consume? Global data centers consumed **415 terawatt-hours (TWh)** of electricity in 2024, representing approximately 1.5% of worldwide electricity demand, according to the International Energy Agency (IEA). ### MARKET SPOTLIGHT

# Africa’s Key Data Centre Markets

## SOUTH AFRICA, NIGERIA, KENYA, EGYPT, AND

## MOROCCO

* * *

Market Overview

Africa has received strong attention from the investors, operators, suppliers, and other stakeholders in the data centre industry.

The FLAP-D markets (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin) are facing acute land and capacity constraints, with Amsterdam and Dublin introducing restrictive policies for data centres development. We are now seeing hyperscalers and operators demand expand into different regions, including Africa. In the continent, international operators are entering emerging markets and investors are seeking higher returns with fibre, submarine cables and data centre projects. The digital boom driven by the COVID-19 pandemic has grown the connectivity, mobile and internet usage for the entire continent.

Africa has 54 countries. However, the data centre development across the continent could be split into five main hubs: South Africa, Nigeria, Egypt, Morocco, and Kenya. With the onset of hyperscale activity, South Africa became a major destination for data centre development, boasting as the most established market across Africa. We continue to see a range of economic growth, regulated framework, infrastructure, and technological standards across the African countries, with the investors and operators looking to enter new emerging markets.

* * *

* * *

* * *

* * *

MARKET SPOTLIGHT MARKET SPOTLIGHT AFRICA’S KEY DATA CENTRE MARKETS \| SOUTH AFRICA, NIGERIA, KENYA, EGYPT, AND MOROCCO

# Growth Trends in South Africa, Lagos, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco

The COVID-19 pandemic, population sizes and the increasing business demands have contributed to the digital boom seen

across Africa. Nigeria and Kenya, for example, have benefited from governmental policies and initiatives focusing on digital

## transformation that played a major role in the growth of their data centre industries.

From the below percentages, we can see how much the total IT capacities have grown over the last four years (2018 – 2022). Morocco has achieved an annual compound growth rate (CAGR) of 5.13%. Meanwhile, Egypt has achieved a CAGR of 17.33% and in South Africa, it was noted at 42.72%. Lastly, CAGR rates in Nigeria reached 50.58% while Kenya witnessed a CAGR of 84.45%.

The demand for digital services infrastructure in all five countries is clearly visible. Notably Morocco has the lowest and Kenya – the highest percentage of growth from these five countries. In addition, it shows how much progress was made to drive digital transformation and to promote the development of data centres.

In Q1 2023, the five markets mentioned comprised of over 800MW of total IT power. This includes live and operational power as well as the development pipeline. It is important to note that South Africa accounts for over 408MW of the total supply, with the majority of the western hyperscalers deploying in the market.

Johannesburg and Cape Town are currently the only cities in Africa to have fully established cloud regions. Cloud operators including IBM, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon Web Services and Google have all deployed in South Africa. In Nairobi, Lagos, and Cairo hyperscalers have only installed local zones and Morocco is yet to record any public cloud activity to date.

* * *

* * *

* * *

Contrasting the Total Market Capacities of South Africa, Lagos, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco

In terms of the total supply, South Africa is the largest market comprising of 408MW. Nigeria and Egypt are second and third, with 140MW and 118MW respectively. Kenya is close behind with 79MW followed by Morocco with 65MW of supply.

In South Africa, only 41% of the total market capacity has been fully developed and fitted out, indicating significant future growth and expansion potential. For example, in Nigeria, Kenya, Morocco, and Egypt the live IT capacity accounts for a small segment of the total market capacity. Nigeria has 21% of the market capacity fully developed and fitted out, while in Kenya and Morocco, both have 19% each. Egypt on the other hand has the lowest proportion, with only 11% of the market capacity observed as live.

The more development projects are completed and brought online, the more live IT capacity in these countries is expected to increase which will support their digital infrastructure and services.

* * *

IT Capacity Breakdown by Country

but the four countries differ widely in terms of their supply in the pipeline.

* * *

The Changing Landscape of Africa’s Data Centre Scene

Public cloud can be deployed either through a third-party colocation provider or by self-builds. Snapshots of the market compositions by live IT capacity for all five countries show the dominance of colocation.

South Africa is dominated by colocation which comprises over 92% of the market composition. Public cloud is driving the demand in wholesale facilities, with Teraco and Africa Data Centres maintaining 57% and 18% of the live IT power.

Colocation facilities account for 76% of total IT capacity in Nigeria, with Kasi Cloud and 21st Century developing the first wholesale facilities in the market. The current transition from retail to wholesale colocation will continue to attract more large-scale infrastructure investments, such as what we have seen with Kasi Cloud.

Kenya’s colocation capacity contributes to 85% of the total IT capacity. Just like Nigeria, Kenya is also developing its first wholesale facilities that can house hyperscale demand. Once these facilities are fully established, the narrative of the market will change from retail to a wholesale-oriented market.

Even though in Egypt there are currently more self-build (Network & Telco, Financial/Institutional, Systems Integrator/Managed Service Providers) facilities in comparison to retail facilities, colocation capacity plays an important role contributing to 71% of the total IT power. We expect Egypt to follow similar trends seen in Nigeria and Kenya, with wholesale developments announced.

The Moroccan colocation capacity contributes to 37% of the total IT power, with a higher number of self-build than retail facilities. Morocco is still early in its development cycle, but if it was to follow similar trends seen across more established markets in the region, we would expect to see more wholesale colocation developments. When we consider under construction and committed power, we expect the colocation capacity to increase from 37% to 81%.

* * *

Conclusion

The data centre markets in South Africa, Nigeria, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco have all seen varied growth during the last five years. Public Cloud deployments in South Africa have led to significant data centre developments, with wholesale colocation facilities dominating the market share. Nigeria, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco are all in the early stages of their development cycles, either dominated by retail colocation or self-build facilities.

As the most established data centre hub in the region, South Africa comprises the largest live IT capacity and development pipeline capacity, with a strong presence of wholesale facilities serving hyperscale demands. On the other hand, Nigeria and Kenya have experienced recent growth in their data centre markets, with plans to develop wholesale facilities alongside their existing colocation capacities.

Egypt and Morocco are both still in early stages of their development cycles, with either retail colocation or self-build facilities accounting for large segments of the market share. However, recent announcements of wholesale developments in both countries indicates confidence of future demand from operators.

There are various factors which have influenced the increase in demand such as business, population demands and government decisions. With the start of the Covid-19 pandemic, there has been a push towards digital transformation which created a boom for the entire continent, with countries like Nigeria, Kenya, Egypt, and Morocco now shifting their focus towards larger deployments to house cloud operators' demands.

The shift in strategy reflects the recognition of the growing demand for data centre infrastructure in these countries. By investing in colocation and wholesale facilities, they aim to meet the needs of businesses and organizations requiring reliable and scalable data centre services. These developments indicate a positive outlook for the data centre industry as they work towards enhancing their capacity and infrastructure to support the evolving digital landscape.

# Best Data Center Locations 2024

May 28, 2024 \| Dominika Koncewicz

[SUBSCRIBE FOR THE LATEST NEWS AND INSIGHTS](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/blogs/2024/may/best-data-center-locations-2024#)

![data center 09_AdobeStock_252931408](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/-/media/tech/omdia/marketing/blogs/2024/data-center-09_adobestock_252931408.jpeg?rev=eda3a6a269f8401e8d12acfd26b8aea8&extension=webp)

As we progress into 2024, the landscape of [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) locations is rapidly evolving, driven by technological advancements, market demands, and geopolitical considerations. Identifying the best data center locations is crucial for businesses leveraging [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) computing, [edge](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/internet-of-things) computing, [artificial intelligence](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/ai), and other data-intensive applications. This article explores the top-ranked data center locations of 2024, emphasizing why these locations are prime spots and highlighting their global significance.

**The Importance of the Right Data Center Location**

Selecting the best [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) location involves evaluating several key criteria to ensure optimal performance, efficiency, and future growth. Each criterion plays a vital role in determining the suitability of a location for data center operations:

• **Connectivity and Bandwidth**: High-speed internet and multiple [fiber](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) providers and routes are essential for minimizing latency and ensuring reliable data transfer. For instance, Northern Virginia, known as "Data Center Alley," has become a top choice due to its dense [network](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) of [fiber-optic](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) connections, which handle a significant portion of global internet traffic. Another example is the 2Africa subsea cable, recently completed around Africa, which will greatly impact the data center market by vastly improving [connectivity](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) and bandwidth, thereby attracting more investments.

• **Climate and Cooling:** Cooler climates reduce the need for artificial cooling, leading to lower energy costs. Dublin, Ireland, is a prime example, where the naturally cool climate helps minimize cooling expenses, attracting major tech companies like Google and Microsoft. Scandinavia is another ideal [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) location due to its naturally cool climate, which significantly reduces the need for artificial cooling, and tech giants like Google and Facebook have invested heavily in countries like Sweden and Denmark to leverage these advantages.

• **Energy Availability and Sustainability:** Access to renewable energy sources is increasingly crucial for sustainable operations. Frankfurt, Germany, stands out as a key [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) hub with its commitment to renewable energy and stringent environmental regulations, ensuring a steady supply of green power. Southeast Asia is also becoming an attractive data center location due to its increasing adoption of renewable energy sources, with countries like Thailand implementing large-scale solar farms, Malaysia integrating solar and hydroelectric power, and the Philippines advancing wind energy projects to power their data centers.

• **Political and Economic Stability:** Stable regions with clear regulations provide a secure environment for data centers. Singapore is a notable example, offering a politically stable environment and strong regulatory framework, although it now faces space and regulatory constraints pushing new investments to nearby areas like Johor in Malaysia.

• **Disaster Risk:** To ensure uninterrupted operations, locations with a low risk of natural disasters are preferred. Dallas, Texas, is emerging as a data center hotspot due to its relatively minimal risk of earthquakes, floods, and hurricanes, combined with its central location and robust infrastructure.

• **Proximity to End-Users:** Proximity to end-users is a critical factor in selecting [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) locations, as it significantly impacts latency, service delivery, and overall user experience. Data centers strategically positioned near key user bases, such as manufacturing regions or financial districts, can offer faster, more reliable access to data and services. In some cases, however, many [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) workloads are not sensitive to latency and building data center campuses in rural areas is more economical. Workloads like training [AI](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/ai) large language models (LLMs), data aggregation and indexing, web crawling, etc., are well suited for a rural data center campus.

**Prime Locations in North America**

North America, particularly the United States, is the largest market for [data centers](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center), driven by its advanced technological infrastructure and high demand for [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) services. Numerous tech giants and a robust digital economy fuel this dominance, ensuring continual growth and investment in data center facilities.

**Northern Virginia**

Known as "Data Center Alley," Northern Virginia hosts a sizable portion of the world’s internet traffic. The region’s excellent connectivity, competitive energy prices, and favorable tax incentives make it a prime location for US data centers. Due to its strategic positioning and infrastructure, it continues to be a top data center hot spot.

**Dallas, Texas**

Dallas has been a top [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) hot spot and continues to grow due to its central location, excellent connectivity, and relatively minimal risk of natural disasters. The region's competitive energy prices and ample available land also contribute to its attractiveness for [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center)[data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) locations. Texas also produces more renewable energy than any other state, with 146 million MWh in 2023, making up 27% of their energy mix.

**Chicago, Illinois**

Chicago is seen as a top data center location in the US, housing over 70 data centers, with major players like Equinix, Digital Realty, and CyrusOne driving its prominence. Chicago is regarded as a premier [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) location in the US due to its strategic central location, providing low-latency connections across the country, and its robust infrastructure, including reliable power supply and advanced cooling systems essential for data center operations. Additionally, Chicago's extensive [fiber-optic](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) network and proximity to major financial exchanges further enhance its attractiveness as a data center hub, ensuring efficient and reliable data transmission for enterprises.

**Silicon Valley, California**

The home of many tech giants for years, Silicon Valley has always been an appealing market for data centers as many companies prefer to be close to their [IT infrastructure](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it). The market isn’t growing explosively, but demand remains high, which allows data center operators to charge premium rates. The market is somewhat of an enigma as it can be costly to operate there, and earthquake risk is high. Santa Clara is the most sought-after locality as its power rates are 20-25% lower than the rest of the Bay Area.

**Expanding markets in North America**

There are many notable markets that have caught the attention of both [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) and colocation [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) builders. What drives them to these markets is a combination of economics, risk mitigation, and required proximity (or not) to customers. Each of these markets has a combination of appealing characteristics, as detailed in the first section above.

- Atlanta, Georgia
- Phoenix, Arizona
- Austin & San Antonio, Texas
- Salt Lake City, Utah
- Hillsboro, Oregon

**EMEA Data Havens**

The [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) market in Europe is booming, with the FLAP-D region (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin) at its core, characterized by low vacancy rates and robust infrastructure. Global players like Equinix, Digital Realty, and AWS dominate the landscape alongside local companies such as Telehouse, DATA4, Atman or Main cubes. Despite stringent EU energy efficiency regulations and increasing demand for sovereign [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) solutions, the market's growth is significant, ensuring compliance and [security](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cybersecurity) for data residency requirements.

Despite the current density and variety of [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) operators, the ongoing high demand for [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) services is driving further investments and the exploration of new locations, expanding the data center footprint across Europe, Middle East and Africa (MEA). The MEA region is clearly an emerging market with increasing interest from global [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) and [colocation](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) companies.

**Madrid, Spain**

Madrid is rapidly emerging as a significant data center hub in Europe. The city offers a strategic location with excellent [connectivity](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications) to both Europe, MEA, and Latin America from cable landing stations in the south of the country. Madrid's robust infrastructure, favorable climate for natural cooling, and supportive government policies are attracting major [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) investments. Companies like Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Microsoft have announced plans to expand their data center presence in the region.

**Berlin, Germany**

Berlin is rapidly gaining recognition as a prime data center location, following in the footsteps of Frankfurt, which has long been established as Germany's leading data center hub. Berlin's strategic geographical position in central Europe makes it an ideal location for data centers. Compared to Frankfurt, Berlin offers relatively lower real estate and operational costs, making it an attractive alternative for data center investments. The city also provides various incentives for tech and [digital infrastructure](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it) investments, including tax breaks and grants. These incentives make Berlin a cost-effective choice for companies looking to establish or expand their [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) operations in Europe.

**Nairobi, Kenya**

Nairobi is rapidly emerging as a data center hub in Africa. Kenya's strategic location, improving infrastructure, and growing tech industry make Nairobi an attractive destination for data center investments. The Kenyan government's focus on [digital transformation](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it) and supportive policies further boost the city's status as a data center location in Africa. Companies like Africa [Data Centers](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center), IX Africa and Digital Realty have established significant operations in Nairobi.

**Dubai, United Arab Emirates**

Dubai continues to solidify its position as a leading [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) hub in the Middle East. The city's strategic location, advanced infrastructure, and supportive government policies make it an attractive destination for [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) investments. Dubai's commitment to becoming a [smart city](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/smart-buildings-and-energy-infrastructure) and its efforts to diversify the economy through digital transformation are driving the growth of data centers in the region. Major tech companies like Equinix, Oracle and Microsoft have established data center operations in Dubai.

**Asia-Pacific's Rapid Growth**

The Asia-Pacific region, particularly South-East Asia (SEA), is experiencing remarkable growth in the [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) market, driven by rapid [digital transformation](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it), and increasing demand for [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) services. Singapore has long been a prominent data center hub due to its strategic location, robust infrastructure, and favorable business environment. However, the city-state is now facing limitations in terms of land availability and stricter regulations on data center operations and energy consumption, prompting businesses to seek alternatives.

Emerging locations like Johor in Malaysia and Batam in Indonesia are rising stars, offering abundant land, favorable regulatory environments, and proximity to Singapore, making them attractive for [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) investments. Additionally, India is witnessing substantial investments in data centers, fueled by its large population, burgeoning digital economy, and supportive government policies aimed at enhancing [digital infrastructure](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it). These developments are positioning SEA and India as a powerhouse in the global [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) market.

**Untapped Potential in South America**

South America is gradually catching up in the [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) market, spurred by increasing digitalization and a growing demand for [cloud](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) services across the continent. Countries like Brazil, Chile, and Argentina are witnessing new investments to expand their [digital infrastructure](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/enterprise-it). Brazil, in particular, is leading the charge with significant investments in cities like São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro, driven by its large market size and improving [connectivity](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/service-providers-and-communications). Equinix, Ascenty, and Scala are among the many investors in South America's [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) market.

**FAQs About Data Center Locations**

_• Why does the location of a__[data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center)__matter?_

Data centers require 99.999% or more of uptime availability to assure that critical applications, whether it be Facebook or hospital operations. They also need appealing economics of construction and operations to maintain their business. Combined, these required characteristics are all about risk mitigation.

_• How do I choose the best__[data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center)__location for my needs?_

When data center site selectors are looking for an ideal place to build and operate a data center, they a list of all the important attributes they like. Each of those attributes is ranked in terms of importance and weighted so they can give each option an overall value score.

_• What if I need help in the process of realizing my data center requirements?_

First, be careful, as many resources claim to know everything. Second, engage with similar peers who have navigated the same situation. Third, take what you have learned and be sure to assess multiple different companies and engage others in your organization to assess the feedback you receive.

Choosing a [data center](https://omdia.tech.informa.com/advance-your-business/cloud-and-data-center) solution is a long-term commitment that will likely be critical to your company’s operations. Rushing to a decision exposes you and your company to a prominent level of risk.

As of April 14, 2026, there were a reported 4,184 data centers in the United States, the most of any country worldwide. A further 515 were located in the United Kingdom, while 514 were located in Germany.

## What is a data center?

[Data centers](https://www.statista.com/topics/6165/data-centers/) are facilities designed to store and compute vast amounts of data efficiently and securely. Growing in importance amid the rise of [cloud computing](https://www.statista.com/topics/1695/cloud-computing/) and artificial intelligence, data centers form the core infrastructure powering global [digital transformation](https://www.statista.com/topics/6778/digital-transformation/). Modern data centers consist of critical computing hardware such as servers, storage systems, and networking equipment organized into racks, alongside specialized secondary infrastructure providing power, cooling, and security.

## AI data centers

Data centers are vital for artificial intelligence, with the world’s leading technology companies investing vast sums in new facilities across the globe. Purpose-built [AI data centers](https://www.statista.com/topics/13816/ai-data-centers/) provide the immense computing power required to train the most advanced AI models, as well as to process user requests in real time, a task known as inference. Increasing attention has therefore turned to the [location of these powerful facilities](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1622343/global-ai-compute-regions-by-host-and-type/), as governments grow more concerned with AI sovereignty. At the same time, rapid data center expansion has sparked a [global debate over resource use](https://www.statista.com/topics/13369/data-center-sustainability/), including land, energy, and water, as modern facilities begin to strain local infrastructure.

How many data centers are in the US?There are 2002 data centers in the US, including 812 operating and 1150 planned facilities.

What is the largest operating data center in the US?The largest operating data center in the US is IREN Childress (Bitcoin mining), developed by IREN, with a capacity of 750 MW.

What is the largest planned data center in the US?The largest planned data center in the US is Delta Gigasite - Expansion, being developed by Creekstone Energy, with a planned capacity of 9,700 MW.

How many data center developers are active in the US?There are 453 unique data center developers with projects in the US.

1. The African sub-region is witnessing a significant rise in upcoming data centers with countries like Nigeria, Kenya, Egypt, and South Africa contributing as the top markets for the region
